content:
5 min read Just now -- If your React application downloads every component on the first visit, you’re probably shipping more JavaScript than your users actually need. Let’s see how Lazy Loading helps us fix that. Press enter or click to view image in full size Imagine opening a React application that only shows a simple Home page, but behind the scenes it’s already downloading the code for the Dashboard, Admin panel, Profile page, and a bunch of other features you may never visit. That means your users are downloading JavaScript they don’t actually need yet, which leads to a slower initial load and a less responsive experience. This is exactly the problem that Lazy Loading is designed to solve. What is Lazy Loading? Lazy Loading is a simple idea: don’t load something until you actually need it. Instead of downloading every component when your React app starts, you only load the component when the user is about to use it. For example, if a user lands on the Home page, there’s no reason to download the Dashboard or Settings code right away. Those components can wait until the user navigates to them. This approach makes your application’s initial load faster, reduces the amount of JavaScript the browser has to download, and gives users a smoother experience. Let’s See It in Action Now that you know the idea behind Lazy Loading, let’s look at a couple of real examples. Example 1: Lazy Loading a Component Suppose you have a Profile component that users don't need as soon as the application loads. You can lazy load it like this: import React from "react"; const Profile = React.lazy(() => import("./Profile")); function App() { return (

Welcome to My App

); } export default App;With this approach, React waits to download the Profile component until it's actually needed. Example 2: Lazy Loading Routes One of the most common use cases for Lazy Loading is with React Router. Instead of downloading every page when the application starts, each page is loaded only when the user navigates to it. import React from "react"; import { Routes, Route } from "react-router-dom"; const Home = React.lazy(() => import("./pages/Home")); const Dashboard = React.lazy(() => import("./pages/Dashboard")); const Settings = React.lazy(() => import("./pages/Settings"));function App() { return ( } /> } /> } /> ); } export default App; Now, each page is downloaded only when the user visits it, reducing the amount of JavaScript loaded on the first visit. Let’s Break It Down The most important line in both examples is this one: const Profile = React.lazy(() => import("./Profile"));Let’s break it down step by step. React.lazy(...)React.lazy() tells React that this component should be loaded lazily instead of being included in the initial bundle. Inside React.lazy(), we pass a function: () => import("./Profile")Instead of using a regular import, We're using a dynamic import. Unlike a regular import, a dynamic import doesn’t download the component immediately. Instead, it returns a Promise that React resolves only when the component is actually needed. In simple terms, React waits until it needs to render the Profile component, then downloads it and displays it. That’s what makes Lazy Loading possible. Loading a component isn’t instant. For a brief moment, React is still downloading the component. During that time, what should users see? Without handling this loading state, React doesn’t know what to render while it’s waiting for the component to finish downloading. That’s exactly why React introduced Suspense. The Solution: Suspense Suspense lets us display a fallback UI while React is loading a lazy-loaded component. The fallback can be a loading spinner, a skeleton screen, or even a simple "Loading..." message. import React, { Suspense } from "react";const Profile = React.lazy(() => import("./Profile")); function App() { return (

Welcome to My App

Loading Profile...
}> ); } export default App; While React is downloading the Profile component, users will see the fallback UI. As soon as the component is ready, React automatically replaces the fallback with the actual component. Everything looks simple from our side. But behind the scenes, React, the bundler, and the browser are all working together to make this happen. So, what’s actually going on? What’s Happening Behind the Scenes? Now you know how to use Lazy Loading in your React application. So, what actually happens after writing this line? const Dashboard = lazy(() => import("./Dashboard"));The key here is the dynamic import. Unlike a regular import, A dynamic import doesn't download the component immediately. Instead, it tells the bundler that this component can be loaded later. During the build process, the bundler (such as Vite or Webpack) creates a separate JavaScript file, often called a chunk, for that component. So instead of shipping one large JavaScript bundle, your application is split into smaller pieces. When the user finally navigates to the Dashboard page, the browser downloads only the Dashboard chunk, React renders it, and the user never notices what's happening behind the scenes. That’s the combination of Code Splitting and Lazy Loading working together. Think of it this way: 1-Code Splitting creates the chunks. 2-Lazy Loading decides when those chunks should be downloaded. Press enter or click to view image in full size When Should You Use Lazy Loading? Lazy Loading isn’t something you should apply to every component. A good rule of thumb is to use it for components that aren’t needed immediately when the application first loads. Some common examples include: • Routes with React Router • Dashboard and Admin pages • Charts and analytics components • Rich text editors (like CKEditor or TinyMCE) • PDF viewers • Large third-party libraries • Modals that users open occasionally If a component isn’t visible on the first screen, it’s usually a great candidate for Lazy Loading. Conclusion Lazy Loading is a simple but powerful technique for improving your React application’s performance. By using React.lazy() and Suspense, you only load components when they're actually needed, making your app faster and more efficient. The next time you’re building a React app, ask yourself: “Does this component need to be loaded right away?”If not, Lazy Loading is probably the right choice. 🚀
